skill.name: specdd-explain
skill.description: Use when Agent needs to explain a SpecDD spec, target, behavior, or effective spec chain without editing it.
skill.summary: Explain SpecDD specifications, targets, and behaviors in plain language.
skill.author: specdd

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 16 static findings in SKILL.md (79 lines). All findings are false positives: the high-severity weak cryptographic algorithm flag is an SPDX license identifier (Apache-2.0); all 13 medium-severity Ruby/shell backtick execution flags are Markdown code span syntax for file paths and keywords; both low-severity system reconnaissance flags are AI behavioral guidelines about explanation scope. The skill is a read-only documentation tool with no code execution, network access, file modification, or data exfiltration capabilities. No prompt injection or other semantic threats detected.

content.user_title: Explain SpecDD Specifications in Plain Language
content.value_statement: Understanding SpecDD specification contracts can be complex. This skill explains specs, targets, and behaviors in clear, plain language without editing files.
actual_capabilities: Explain SpecDD specs, targets, and behaviors in plain language
Resolve and explain the effective spec chain from bootstrap to target
Distinguish direct spec requirements from inferred behavior
Identify spec scope, ownership, boundaries, and dependencies
Clarify write authority and public contract constraints
Highlight underspecified behavior and missing context
limitations: Cannot edit or modify spec files under any circumstances
Cannot create new specs or implementations
Requires an existing SpecDD project with bootstrap files
Does not execute CLI tools or external commands

use_cases.title: Understanding SpecDD Contracts
use_cases.description: New team members use this skill to understand existing SpecDD specs and how they govern feature development.
use_cases.target_user: New developers joining a SpecDD project

use_cases.title: Spec-Guided Code Review
use_cases.description: Reviewers use SpecDD specs to verify that implementation changes align with documented behavior and constraints.

use_cases.title: Project Architecture Analysis
use_cases.description: Technical leads explore the full spec chain to understand project architecture, dependencies, and behavioral boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Single Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain what this spec means: [paste spec content or path]
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to understand what a specific spec file governs

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Target Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the spec requirements for [target path or feature name]
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to understand what contracts govern a specific feature

prompt_templates.title: Resolve and Explain Spec Chain
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the effective spec chain for [target path] starting from bootstrap
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to see how bootstrap files cascade to govern a target

prompt_templates.title: Compare Spec Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the difference between what is specified and what is inferred for [target]? List boundaries, open points, and underspecified behavior.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to identify gaps between explicit specs and actual implementation

output_examples.input: Explain what the spec at specs/auth/login.md means
output_examples.output: The spec at specs/auth/login.md governs the login feature. It defines the login form behavior, validation rules, and authentication flow. It requires the username and password fields. It forbids storing plain text credentials. It depends on the session management module.

output_examples.input: What are the boundaries for the payment module?
output: The payment spec defines these boundaries: It handles credit card and digital wallet payments only. It does not cover refunds or chargebacks. It requires third-party PCI compliance. It forbids logging full card numbers. It depends on the order management spec.

output_examples.input: List underspecified behavior for the notification feature
output: Underspecified behavior in the notification spec: Retry logic on delivery failure is not defined. Rate limits for push notifications are missing. The spec says emails must be sent but does not define the email template structure.

best_practices: Always resolve the bootstrap chain first to ensure the spec context is accurate
Read the exact spec text before explaining instead of relying on summaries or memory
Label inferred behavior clearly to distinguish specifications from assumptions
anti_patterns: Editing spec files while explaining them - this skill is read-only by design
Inferring authority from file naming conventions not defined in the bootstrap
Expanding into unrelated sibling specs without an explicit user request

faq.question: What is a SpecDD spec?
faq.answer: A SpecDD spec is a contract file that defines behavior, scope, ownership, and constraints for a feature or module.

faq.question: Does this skill edit files?
faq.answer: No. This skill only explains specs. It never edits, creates, or modifies files.

faq.question: What is the difference between a spec and a target?
faq.answer: A spec is a contract file. A target is the feature, module, or behavior governed by one or more specs.

faq.question: What is the bootstrap chain?
faq.answer: The bootstrap chain is a set of files that define project-level rules. It starts from .specdd/bootstrap.md and extends with project and local overrides.

faq.question: Can this skill work without a bootstrap file?
faq.answer: No. A root .specdd/bootstrap.md file is required for the skill to function correctly.

faq.question: What does effective spec chain mean?
faq.answer: The effective spec chain is the ordered list of specs from the content root to the target that govern a specific feature or module.
